content:
# Mark Chapter 8 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), folios 36r–36v (PDF pages 71–72) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_008_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_008_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Witness:** Codex Vaticanus (B, 03) — consulted via [DigiVatLib](https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209)

---

¹ Around that time another huge crowd had gathered, and once again they had nothing to eat. Jesus called his disciples over and said,

² "My heart goes out to these people. They've been with me three days now and have nothing left to eat.

³ If I send them home hungry, they'll collapse along the way—and some of them have traveled a long distance."

⁴ His disciples responded, "Where could anyone find enough food to feed them way out here?"

⁵ "How many loaves do you have?" he asked. "Seven," they said.

⁶ He told the crowd to sit down on the ground. He took the seven loaves, gave thanks to God, broke them, and handed them to his disciples to distribute. They passed them out to the crowd.

⁷ They also had a few small fish. He blessed them and had those passed around as well.

⁸ Everyone ate until they were full. Afterward, the disciples picked up seven large baskets of leftover pieces.

⁹ About four thousand people had eaten. Then Jesus sent them home.

¹⁰ He immediately got into the boat with his disciples and sailed to the area of Dalmanutha.

¹¹ The Pharisees showed up and started arguing with him, demanding a miraculous sign from heaven to prove himself.

¹² Jesus let out a deep sigh and said, "Why does this generation demand a sign? I tell you the truth: this generation will get no sign at all."

¹³ He turned away from them, got back in the boat, and crossed to the other side.

¹⁴ The disciples had forgotten to bring food—they had only one loaf of bread in the boat.

¹⁵ "Be on your guard," Jesus warned them. "Watch out for the yeast of the Pharisees and the yeast of Herod."

¹⁶ They started arguing among themselves: "He's saying this because we forgot to bring bread."

¹⁷ Jesus knew exactly what they were thinking. "Why are you worried about bread?" he said. "Don't you get it yet? Don't you understand? Are your hearts that hard?

¹⁸ You have eyes—can't you see? You have ears—can't you hear? Have you already forgotten?

¹⁹ When I broke five loaves for five thousand people, how many baskets of leftovers did you collect?" "Twelve," they said.

²⁰ "And when I broke seven loaves for four thousand, how many baskets?" "Seven," they said.

²¹ "Then how can you still not understand?"

²² They arrived at Bethsaida, where some people brought a blind man to Jesus and begged him to touch him.

²³ Jesus took the man by the hand and led him outside the village. He spit on the man's eyes, placed his hands on him, and asked, "Can you see anything?"

²⁴ The man looked around and said, "I can see people, but they look like trees walking around."

²⁵ Jesus placed his hands on the man's eyes again. This time the man looked hard, and his sight was fully restored. He could see everything sharp and clear.

²⁶ Jesus sent him home with the instruction, "Don't go back into the village."

²⁷ Jesus and his disciples set out for the villages near Caesarea Philippi. On the road, he asked them, "Who are people saying I am?"

²⁸ They answered, "Some say John the Baptist. Others say Elijah. Others say one of the prophets."

²⁹ "But what about you?" he pressed. "Who do you say I am?" Peter spoke up: "You are the Messiah."

³⁰ Jesus gave them strict orders to tell no one.

³¹ Then he began to teach them something they weren't ready for: the Son of Man would have to suffer terribly, be rejected by the religious establishment—the elders, chief priests, and scholars—be killed, and then rise three days later.

³² He said all this in plain language, holding nothing back. Peter pulled him aside and started telling him off.

³³ But Jesus turned around, looked at his disciples, and rebuked Peter sharply: "Get behind me, Satan! You're thinking like a human being, not like God."

³⁴ Then he called the crowd over, along with his disciples, and said, "If any of you want to follow me, you must give up your own way, pick up your cross, and come with me.

³⁵ If you try to hold on to your life, you'll lose it. But if you let go of your life for my sake and for the sake of the good news, you'll find it.

³⁶ What good does it do to win the whole world if it costs you your very self?

³⁷ What could you ever trade to get yourself back?

³⁸ If anyone is embarrassed to be associated with me and my message in this unfaithful, sinful generation, the Son of Man will be embarrassed of them when he arrives in the glory of his Father with the holy angels."

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

1. **The disciples' stunning obtuseness (vv. 14-21):** They have just witnessed two miraculous feedings—5,000 and 4,000—and they're worried about forgetting to bring bread? Jesus's exasperation is palpable. His series of questions in vv. 17-21 is the longest rebuke of the disciples in Mark. The man who opened deaf ears cannot open their understanding.

2. **"People... like trees walking" (v. 24):** This is the only two-stage miracle in the Gospels. The partial vision functions as a perfect metaphor for Peter's confession that follows: Peter sees truly (Jesus is the Messiah) but not clearly (he rejects the path of suffering). Mark is a master storyteller.

3. **"You are the Messiah" (v. 29):** I render ΧΡΙΣΤΟΣ as "Messiah" rather than "Christ" because the Greek word translates the Hebrew מָשִׁיחַ (mashiach, "anointed one"). In this Jewish context, Peter is making a specific claim about Jesus's identity as Israel's promised king—not using a proper name.

4. **"Get behind me, Satan" (v. 33):** The most shocking rebuke in the Gospels. The same Simon who just made the right confession is now playing the role of the tempter—trying to divert Jesus from the cross. Jesus sees through it instantly. The temptation to power without suffering is always satanic.

5. **"Pick up your cross" (v. 34):** The first audience hearing this would have understood it literally—crosses lined Roman roads. This is not a metaphor for minor inconveniences. It means: be willing to walk to your execution.

6. **"This unfaithful, sinful generation" (v. 38):** The word "adulterous" (μοιχαλίς) is prophetic language—Israel as God's unfaithful spouse (cf. Hosea). I rendered it "unfaithful" to preserve the covenantal dimension.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
